steps.title: Recognize the key relationship
steps.content: As the sample size $n$ increases, the denominator $\sqrt{n}$ in the margin of error formula grows, making the overall margin of error smaller. This is the fundamental principle: larger samples produce more precise estimates with smaller margins of error.

steps.title: Apply to the two sample sizes
steps.content: Comparing $n = 120$ to $n = 240$: since $\sqrt{240} \gt  \sqrt{120}$, the margin of error is smaller for the larger sample. The 240-student sample will have a lower margin of error than the 120-student sample.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A - The reported margin of error would be lower.: Larger sample size decreases the margin of error because $$ME \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$. ✓
  • Choice B - The reported margin of error would be higher.: This is backwards - more data means more precision, not less. ✗
  • Choice C - The reported average number of minutes would be lower.: Sample size does not systematically push the estimated mean lower. ✗
  • Choice D - The reported average number of minutes would be higher.: Sample size does not systematically push the estimated mean higher. ✗
A is correct.
